quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Kinetic molecular theory"?

options.text: An acid that can donate more than one proton: diprotic (H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃) or triprotic (H₃PO₄)
Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)
A strong dipole-dipole interaction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on another N, O, or F atom
A reaction that absorbs heat from the surroundings; ΔH > 0; products are higher in energy than reactants

quiz_questions.explanation: "Kinetic molecular theory" is defined as: Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Phase diagram"?

options.text: A strong dipole-dipole interaction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on another N, O, or F atom
The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu
Two or more reactants combine to form one product: A + B → AB (e.g., 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl)
A graph showing the stable phase of a substance as a function of temperature and pressure; includes triple point and critical point

quiz_questions.explanation: "Phase diagram" is defined as: A graph showing the stable phase of a substance as a function of temperature and pressure; includes triple point and critical point. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Triple point"?

options.text: The temperature and pressure at which all three phases coexist in equilibrium (water: 0.01°C, 611.7 Pa)
Adding a species shifts equilibrium away from it; removing a species shifts equilibrium toward it
Two or more reactants combine to form one product: A + B → AB (e.g., 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl)
An equation relating reaction rate to reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ; exponents determined experimentally

quiz_questions.explanation: "Triple point" is defined as: The temperature and pressure at which all three phases coexist in equilibrium (water: 0.01°C, 611.7 Pa). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Critical point"?

options.text: The ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients; temperature-dependent
The temperature and pressure above which the liquid and gas phases are indistinguishable (supercritical fluid)
One compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances: AB → A + B (e.g., 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂)
The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Critical point" is defined as: The temperature and pressure above which the liquid and gas phases are indistinguishable (supercritical fluid). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Heat of vaporization"?

options.text: A reaction that releases heat to the surroundings; ΔH < 0; products are lower in energy than reactants
The energy required to convert 1 mole of liquid to gas at the boiling point; reflects the strength of IMFs being overcome
An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product
The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Heat of vaporization" is defined as: The energy required to convert 1 mole of liquid to gas at the boiling point; reflects the strength of IMFs being overcome. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)"?

options.text: Atomic number (Z)
Indicator
Kinetic molecular theory
Equilibrium vs. completion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Kinetic molecular theory".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A graph showing the stable phase of a substance as a function of temperature and pressure; includes triple point and critical point"?

options.text: Phase diagram
First law of thermodynamics
Mole
Lewis acid/base

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Phase diagram".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The temperature and pressure at which all three phases coexist in equilibrium (water: 0.01°C, 611.7 Pa)"?

options.text: Ka (acid dissociation constant)
Rate-determining step
Triple point
pKa

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Triple point".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The temperature and pressure above which the liquid and gas phases are indistinguishable (supercritical fluid)"?

options.text: Chromatography
Kw (autoionization constant)
Hess's Law
Critical point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Critical point".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The energy required to convert 1 mole of liquid to gas at the boiling point; reflects the strength of IMFs being overcome"?

options.text: Collision theory
Heat of vaporization
Alloy
Hybridization

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Heat of vaporization".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about States of Matter is accurate?

options.text: Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) and strong bases (NaOH, KOH) dissociate completely; weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka or Kb values.
States of Matter (solid, liquid, gas) are determined by the balance between kinetic energy of particles and the strength of intermolecular forces.
Atomic radius decreases left to right (increasing Zeff) and increases top to bottom (more electron shells).
For aA + bB → products, rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ, where m and n are reaction orders (determined experimentally, NOT from coefficients).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes States of Matter as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of States of Matter?

options.text: Gases have high KE and weak IMFs (particles far apart, random motion); liquids have moderate KE and significant IMFs; solids have low KE and strong IMFs (fixed positions).
Stoichiometric calculations convert between grams, moles, and particles using molar mass and mole ratios from balanced equations.
Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.
Calorimetry measures ΔH experimentally: in coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q = mcΔT; in bomb calorimetry (constant volume), q = CcalΔT.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes States of Matter as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about States of Matter?

options.text: Entropy increases when: solids melt or liquids vaporize, gases expand, temperature increases, or the number of particles increases.
Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) and strong bases (NaOH, KOH) dissociate completely; weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka or Kb values.
Q (reaction quotient) compared to K predicts the direction a reaction will shift.
Phase changes (melting, boiling, sublimation, condensation, freezing, deposition) occur at specific temperatures where energy input/removal overcomes or establishes IMFs.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes States of Matter as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of States of Matter?

options.text: Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering) depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.
States of Matter (solid, liquid, gas) are determined by the balance between kinetic energy of particles and the strength of intermolecular forces.
Equilibrium Constants quantify the position of equilibrium.
The 5% approximation simplifies calculations when [HA]₀/Ka > 400.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes States of Matter as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on States of Matter?

options.text: Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
Metallic Bonding and Structures describes how metal atoms share their valence electrons in a 'sea of electrons' that surrounds a lattice of positive metal cations.
Gases have high KE and weak IMFs (particles far apart, random motion); liquids have moderate KE and significant IMFs; solids have low KE and strong IMFs (fixed positions).
Each elementary step has a molecularity (unimolecular, bimolecular, rarely termolecular) that directly determines its rate law.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes States of Matter as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of States of Matter?

options.text: Antifreeze (ethylene glycol) in car radiators both lowering freezing point and raising boiling point of the coolant solution
Refrigerators and air conditioners: external work (electricity) is required to decrease entropy locally (cool the interior) while increasing total universal entropy
Freeze-drying (lyophilization) of food and pharmaceuticals using sublimation under reduced pressure to remove water while preserving structure and nutrients
Industrial chemical production (ammonia synthesis: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃) using stoichiometry to optimize raw material usage and minimize waste

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of States of Matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of States of Matter?

options.text: Fluorine's extreme electronegativity making it the most reactive nonmetal, used in Teflon (PTFE) coatings and uranium enrichment (UF₆)
Table salt (NaCl) as a classic ionic compound: Na donates one electron to Cl, forming Na⁺Cl⁻ with a crystal lattice structure and high melting point (801°C)
Pressure cookers raising the boiling point of water by increasing pressure, cooking food faster at higher temperatures
Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of States of Matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is States of Matter applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Antacid tablet surface area experiments: crushed tablets dissolve faster than whole tablets because increased surface area increases reaction rate
Supercritical CO₂ used as a green solvent in decaffeination of coffee and dry cleaning, replacing toxic organic solvents
Drug elimination from the body often following first-order kinetics: dosing intervals based on half-life to maintain therapeutic blood levels
Biological research: phosphate buffer (H₂PO₄⁻/HPO₄²⁻, pKa = 7.20) commonly used in cell culture media to maintain physiological pH

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of States of Matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of States of Matter?

options.text: Freeze-drying (lyophilization) of food and pharmaceuticals using sublimation under reduced pressure to remove water while preserving structure and nutrients
Carbonated beverages: dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a diprotic acid giving soft drinks their tangy taste (pH ~3-4)
Catalytic converters using Pt, Pd, and Rh heterogeneous catalysts to lower Ea for converting exhaust pollutants (CO, NOₓ) to harmless products
Industrial removal of products: in esterification (alcohol + acid ⇌ ester + water), removing water with molecular sieves shifts equilibrium toward ester, increasing yield

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of States of Matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows States of Matter in practice?

options.text: Haber process optimization (N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃) using high pressure and moderate temperature to shift equilibrium toward ammonia production for fertilizers
Pressure cookers raising the boiling point of water by increasing pressure, cooking food faster at higher temperatures
Hemoglobin-oxygen binding equilibrium shifting in lungs (high O₂, favoring HbO₂) vs. tissues (low O₂, favoring O₂ release)
Soda carbonation: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq) equilibrium maintained under pressure; opening the bottle shifts equilibrium toward gas release (fizzing)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of States of Matter.
